⭐ EDA Dashboard · GitHub REST API

What makes a GitHub
repository go viral?

Exploratory analysis of 500 most-starred public repositories — uncovering patterns in language trends, community dynamics, topic ecosystems, and star prediction.

Q1 · Star Distribution How are GitHub stars distributed?
Power-law distribution
Key Finding
Stars follow a log-normal / power-law distribution — a large majority of repos cluster between 44K–80K stars, while only a tiny elite exceeds 200K. The KDE curve confirms a long right tail, meaning a few repositories completely dominate in terms of popularity. The notched boxplot reveals that C++ and TypeScript repos achieve higher median stars, while JavaScript — despite having the most repos — shows wider variance.
Q2 · Language Dominance Which languages dominate top repositories?
Python leads at 23.8%
Key Finding
Python (23.8%) dominates the repository share, followed by TypeScript (18.4%) and JavaScript (13.6%) — together accounting for over 55% of all repositories. However, the diverging bar chart tells a different story: Markdown and Batchfile repos score far above the global median in stars, confirming that educational and documentation repos punch above their weight in popularity, despite being fewer in count. Go and Rust repos also outperform the median, reflecting their high-quality community standards.
Q3 · Metric Correlations How do engagement metrics relate?
Stars ↔ Forks r = 0.61
Key Finding
Stars and Watchers are perfectly correlated (r = 1.00) — GitHub counts them as near-identical metrics, so Watchers was excluded from the model to avoid data leakage. Stars ↔ Forks shows r = 0.61 — a strong positive relationship confirming that popular repos get forked more. Forks ↔ Contributors (r = 0.40) indicates that collaborative repos attract more community forks. Repo age has a very weak relationship with stars — newer repos can compete with older ones if the content is relevant.
Q4 · Timeline Growth How has open-source evolved over time?
AI-driven revival 2023–25
Key Finding
Repository creation peaked in 2013–2016 during the early GitHub adoption wave, dipped through 2017–2022, then saw a sharp AI/LLM-driven revival from 2023 onwards. Repos created in 2014 show the highest average stars — they had both time to accumulate stars AND were created during peak momentum. The stacked area chart confirms Python's rise post-2018 and TypeScript's explosion post-2020, while JavaScript's dominance has relatively flattened. The heatmap clearly shows Go and Rust appearing only from 2015+ but quickly gaining traction.
Q5 · Topic Ecosystem What topics define popular open-source projects?
AI topics surging
Key Finding
javascript and python are the most common tags by volume (69 and 68 repos respectively), reflecting the language ecosystem. However, ai, llm, and chatgpt are the fastest-rising topics — appearing exclusively in 2022–2025 repos — signalling a fundamental shift in what the community finds valuable. hacktoberfest appears in 43 repos, highlighting the event-driven contribution culture. The bubble chart shows awesome-list and machine-learning as the largest non-language bubbles, confirming that curated resource lists dominate in popularity.
Q6 · Age vs Popularity Does repository age predict star count?
Weak positive r ≈ 0.1–0.2
Key Finding
The OLS regression shows a statistically significant but weak positive relationship between repo age and stars (r ≈ 0.1–0.2, p < 0.05) — older repos have had more time but age alone does not determine success. The violin plot reveals that 2–5 year old repos have the widest spread, meaning the era of creation (2019–2022) matters more than raw age. Most strikingly, repos under 2 years old already reach 44K+ stars — AI and LLM projects can go completely viral within months, bypassing the age premium entirely.
Q7 · Top Repositories Who are the absolute top repos?
build-your-own-x leads at 490K ⭐
Key Finding
build-your-own-x leads with 490K stars — remarkably, it is a documentation and tutorial repo, not a code framework. This confirms that content and educational value drive virality more than technical complexity. The parallel coordinates chart shows freeCodeCamp uniquely dominates in contributors (6,000+) and open issues — it operates as a full-scale community platform. The top 10 is almost entirely educational, resource aggregation, or awesome-list repos, with very few pure code libraries — signalling what the developer community truly values.
Q8 · Community Participation Which languages drive community engagement?
C++ leads in contributors
Key Finding
C++ and Go repos attract the highest median contributors, despite being fewer in number — their communities are deeply committed and collaborative. The bubble chart reveals TypeScript repos have both high avg stars and high contributors, making it the most well-rounded language ecosystem. JavaScript has the most repos but only moderate contributors per repo — quantity over depth. The plasma colormap (avg forks) shows C and C++ repos are disproportionately forked relative to their star counts, indicating high reuse in downstream projects.
Model · Linear Regression Can we predict repository star count?
Features: forks · contributors · issues · age
R² (Raw Model)
~0.3
R² (Log Model)
~0.35
Top Feature
Forks
Test Set
100
Model Interpretation
Forks is the single strongest predictor of star count, confirming our EDA finding (r = 0.61). The more a repo is forked, the more visible and starred it becomes — a self-reinforcing cycle. Contributors is the second strongest feature — collaborative repos attract wider audiences and more organic discovery. The log-transformed model outperforms the raw model because it better handles the power-law distribution of stars, reducing the distorting influence of extreme outliers like freeCodeCamp (442K stars). Residuals are centred near zero with no systematic bias, though heteroscedasticity is visible at high predicted values — a known limitation of linear regression on power-law targets. A future improvement would use Random Forest or Gradient Boosting to capture the non-linear interactions between features.
